title: Rebuild of Evangelion In Shinjis Image
display_title: Rebuild of Evangelion In Shinjis Image
wikitext:
[]: (Re)build of Evangelion - In Shinji's Image             Quote Post by max_mighty » Fri Jun 04, 2010 2:56 am Hey fellers. I want to see if there is any desire/demand for discussion about the Rebuild series as a sub-story within the Evangelion storyline. As RoE 2.0: You Can (Not) Advance is becoming more available with English subtitles, I see that there has been much discussion about the storyline with some very interesting points being made as well as some pretty inane babble from the more casual posters. I welcome everyone. After all, this forum should be open to all those who enjoy the Evangelion series as a member of any category: fanboys/fangirls/those who like to dress up/those who simply enjoy the show. Having said that, I am a bit of a geek for Evangelion. I'll get to it. After years of occasional watching/re-watching and reading about the references to real-life folklore on the show (spear of destiny/longinus, dead sea scrolls, and various other references to Christianity), I am still absorbed by how rich and abundant the story behind this series is. At the same time, I find that there is a limit to how much we can attribute to "the truth". I honestly don't think the creators of Evangelion wrote the series with the ending in mind, and as such, their ambiguity served many purposes. Consistent with this opinion, I don't think the Rebuild series was meant to answer the questions we seem to beg. In a storm of speculation, I think we are forgetting the big picture. At the end of the original series, there is a lot of emphasis on Shinji, Shinji, Shinji. This, to some, was less than desirable. Who cared about Shinji? It turned out Shinji was left with the power to decide the fate of the world. Great. If any of you humored the conclusion of the last episode (not End of Evangelion), basically, Shinji decides on something like.. happiness. All the characters appear before him, clapping, congratulating him. What a psychotic, empty white-room ending to an otherwise dark, romantic series. I go crazy just watching it. Well, I think the Rebuild series is just that. I think the odd differences in plot are due in some part to his mixing up memories, or reinterpretation of the events that led to the end of the world. It's Shinji's idea of the world, he has the power of narration. I've always thought that the ending in End of Evangelion, where Shinji and Asuka are, basically, left to revive the human species through procreation, was a just product of Shinji's imagination. My point is, Evangelion is not so much about the intricacies between Seele/Nerv, Kaoru/Rei, Adam/Lillith. It's an existential piece about humanity, through the experiences of a lonely, completely ordinary person.

[]:             Quote Post by Lucretius » Fri Jun 04, 2010 3:23 am I'd like to post an exasperated image gif here, but I don't want to get banned. So I'll go with the happy Asuka emoticon 'cause it's cute.

[]:             Quote Post by Merridian » Fri Jun 04, 2010 8:54 am I think this kinda fits in with “sequel theory” max_mighty wrote: I think the odd differences in plot are due in some part to his mixing up memories, or reinterpretation of the events that led to the end of the world. It's Shinji's idea of the world, he has the power of narration.

At the end of the original series, there is a lot of emphasis on Shinji, Shinji, Shinji. This, to some, was less than desirable. Who cared about Shinji? It turned out Shinji was left with the power to decide the fate of the world. Great. If any of you humored the conclusion of the last episode (not End of Evangelion), basically, Shinji decides on something like.. happiness. All the characters appear before him, clapping, congratulating him. What a psychotic, empty white-room ending to an otherwise dark, romantic series. I go crazy just watching it. Well, I think the Rebuild series is just that. I think the odd differences in plot are due in some part to his mixing up memories, or reinterpretation of the events that led to the end of the world. It's Shinji's idea of the world, he has the power of narration. I've always thought that the ending in End of Evangelion, where Shinji and Asuka are, basically, left to revive the human species through procreation, was a just product of Shinji's imagination. My point is, Evangelion is not so much about the intricacies between Seele/Nerv, Kaoru/Rei, Adam/Lillith. It's an existential piece about humanity, through the experiences of a lonely, completely ordinary person.

[]:             Quote Post by sephirotic » Fri Jun 04, 2010 9:36 am No offense but even tough you have a good start, you still need to study evangelion a little better. Evangelion is a psychanalitic simbolist piece about depression made (almost) entirely centered on the experiences of it's solely Creator who experiencied itself a classical clinical 4 year depression and weaster psychanalitical process, and decided to develop deeply characters based on his experiences and set them on a cool-nerd sci-fi/mythological scenario. For starters you made a common mistake for those who are getting deep into eva: Shinji and Asuka AREN´T the only survivals in the EOE, you should rewatch and give a time reading the wiki here on Evageek, is a great source of reliable compiled and detailed information of what the hardcore fandom of the series could decompile of it... To help understand even better eva, it's read the few information we have about it's creator: Hideaki Anno Cheers.

[]: Re: (Re)build of Evangelion - In Shinji's Image             Quote Post by NAveryW » Fri Jun 04, 2010 2:40 pm max_mighty wrote: I've always thought that the ending in End of Evangelion, where Shinji and Asuka are, basically, left to revive the human species through procreation, was a just product of Shinji's imagination.

[]:             Quote Post by 0x2eleven » Sat Jun 05, 2010 5:09 am sephirotic wrote: No offense but even tough you have a good start, you still need to study evangelion a little better. Evangelion is a psychanalitic simbolist piece about depression made (almost) entirely centered on the experiences of it's solely Creator who experiencied itself a classical clinical 4 year depression and weaster psychanalitical process, and decided to develop deeply characters based on his experiences and set them on a cool-nerd sci-fi/mythological scenario. For starters you made a common mistake for those who are getting deep into eva: Shinji and Asuka AREN´T the only survivals in the EOE, you should rewatch and give a time reading the wiki here on Evageek, is a great source of reliable compiled and detailed information of what the hardcore fandom of the series could decompile of it...

[]:             Quote Post by Heromaster111 » Sat Jun 05, 2010 7:34 am Whoever came up with the crazy idea of Adam and Eve ending for EoE I love you.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Sat Jun 05, 2010 8:16 am Heromaster111 wrote: the crazy idea of Adam and Eve ending for EoE

[]:             Quote Post by zachalsorises » Wed Jun 09, 2010 5:31 am [quote="sephirotic#360897"]For starters you made a common mistake for those who are getting deep into eva Shinji and Asuka AREN´T the only survivals in the EOE, you should rewatch and give a time reading the wiki here on Evageek, is a great source of reliable compiled and detailed information of what the hardcore fandom of the series could decompile of it... To help understand even better eva, it's read the few information we have about it's creator Hideaki Anno Cheers.[/quote] Actually, it's stated pretty much point blank in episode 26 that Shinji can create any world he wants. We then hang out in a possible Shinji world for about five minutes. (Then an entire manga series was devoted to it.) So it seems pretty well explained that he did, canonically, have that power. Also, the Evageeks wiki is far from infallible. I've rewatched EoE recently and I've read the "Misconceptions" wiki - there's nothing to suggest that Shinji and Asuka are NOT the last two (hyper-dysfunctional) people around after Third Impact. It's obviously open to interpretation, but the Adam/Eve motif is pretty clear. I agree with the probably-handsome 0x2eleven, Eva is meant to be discussed and interpreted. This isn't science.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Wed Jun 09, 2010 11:25 am Actually, it's stated pretty much point blank in episode 26 that Shinji can create any world he wants. We then hang out in a possible Shinji world for about five minutes. (Then an entire manga series was devoted to it.) So it seems pretty well explained that he did, canonically, have that power.

[]:             Quote Post by Tycho » Mon Jul 05, 2010 12:15 am I'm buying more the sequel (could very well be a prequel too!) thing than "'tis just a dream". Also, I don't think Shinji decided on happiness in Ep26, he doesn't seem to have the power to make it all happy for everyone, he seems simply to accept his existence, and is happy as a consequence of that. All the questions, and people, and mental representations and those alternate reality sequences are what help him in coming to terms with existing. AuraTwilight wrote: Er, no. Yui outright says before she departs that "Anyone can come back", so long as they desire to. Basically, everyone who isn't suicidal will eventually rematerialize. Shinji and Asuka are just the only ones we see, perhaps because they're the only two who landed near to each other and Shinji is the focus.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Mon Jul 05, 2010 2:56 am I know this has been "debunked", but is it really that far-fetched to think Shinji and Asuka are there post 3I because they were inside evas? Well Shinji is there because he had a privileged spot deciding what would happen (and EVA01 kinda decides to leave for the stars ftw!), but Asuka... can't being inside an eva protect you from an "anti-atfield", no matter how trashed it is? Kyoko had gone all touchy on Asuka and all... and we never see her turning into LCL... (And the Asuka that Shinji interacts with during 3I can very well be just the Asuka that exists in Shinji's mind)
